Ecology and GIS Synergy: An Example Utilizing an Open Source Dataset¶

In this post, I aim to explore the interconnection between ecology and Geographic Information Systems (GIS) through a self-initiated project that analyzes an open-source dataset made available by Stichting Bargerveen. This extensive dataset contains more than 108 thousands entries of different taxa across four countries link.

The initial step in conducting analyses based on free data sources is to clearly define the research objectives. Since such data are often gathered for alternative purposes that are frequently unspecified, it is crucial to establish a methodology to avoid misleading results. In this case, the objective is to evaluate how species community composition is influenced by distance and landscape structure, using Carabidae as a model taxon in The Netherlands.

In [1]:
from shapely.geometry import Polygon,MultiPolygon, Point, LineString
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd
import altair as alt

from string import Template

import numpy as np
import folium

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score

27535 rows × 5 columns

Ecology serves as the foundational basis for this analysis. The central questions addressed in this study are: How can we effectively evaluate these influences? How can we compare community composition? While species diversity is the most straightforward and widely utilized method for comparison, this analysis aims to explore the variations in species composition across different sites. Specifically, the focus will be on examining species similarity, species replacement, and nestedness.

The dataset includes point observations of specimens collected from pitfall traps, so my first step was to find the locations of these traps. Next, I organized the observations to create a dataset showing the different species found at each location. To ensure I had a good representation and to reduce noise from yearly changes, I decided to focus on observations from 2023. This year offered enough data points and a good spatial distribution.

In [5]:
df_year = gdf.groupby(['year'],as_index=False)['geometry'].unique()
df_year['number_locations'] = df_year['geometry'].apply(lambda x: len(x))

# Sample data
data = df_year
data.drop('geometry',axis=1,inplace=True)

# Column to highlight
highlight_col = '2023'

# Create bar chart with conditional color
chart = alt.Chart(data).mark_bar().encode(
    x=alt.X('year:N', title=None),
    y=alt.Y('number_locations:Q', title=None),
    color=alt.condition(
        alt.datum.year == highlight_col,  
        alt.value('red'),                  
        alt.value('lightgrey')                 
    )
).properties(
    title=f"Number locations per year",
    width=400,
    height=300
)

chart.show()

gdf_2023 = gdf[gdf['year']=='2023']
df_sp = gdf_2023.groupby(['geometry'],as_index=False)['species'].unique()
df_sp['site'] = list(range(1,len(df_sp)+1))
gdf_sp = gpd.GeoDataFrame(df_sp,geometry=df_sp.geometry)



m = folium.Map(location=[gdf_2023.geometry.y.mean(), gdf_2023.geometry.x.mean()],zoom_start=7, control_scale=True)



for point in gdf_2023['geometry'].unique():
    gdf_temp = gdf_2023[gdf_2023['geometry']==point]
    html_table = gdf_temp.groupby(['species']).size()\
    .to_frame("Number of specimens")\
    .to_html(classes="table table-striped", index=True)


    #___
    html_content = """
    <style>
    table, th, td {
          border: 1px solid black;
          border-radius: 1px;
          padding-top: 5px;
          padding-bottom: 10px;
          padding-left: 15px;
          padding-right: 20px;
        }
        
    h1 {
        text-align: center;
        color: Green;
        font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
    }
    
    summary {
        background-color: #007BFF;
        color: white;
        padding: 5px;
        border-radius: 4px;
        cursor: pointer;
        font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
    }
    
    summary:hover {
        background-color: #0056b3;
        margin: 5px 0;
    }
    
    </style>
    
    
    
    <details>
      <summary>Table</summary>
        <table>$html_table</table>
    </details>
    
    """
    
    template = Template(html_content)
    html_content = template.substitute(html_table=html_table)

    #___

    
    # Wrap HTML in an IFrame so Folium can render it properly
    iframe = folium.IFrame(html=html_content, width=650, height=650,)
    popup=folium.Popup(iframe,width='100%',sticky=True)

    folium.Marker(location=[point.y,point.x],
                  popup = popup,
                  tooltip='click here'
                 ).add_to(m)


    
folium.plugins.Fullscreen(
    position="topright",
    title="Expand me",
    title_cancel="Exit me",
    force_separate_button=True,
).add_to(m)

# <h1>$site</h1>

Out[6]:
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Species similarity refers to the extent of shared species between two biological assemblages. Conversely, species replacement, commonly known as turnover, indicates the concurrent gain and loss of species as a result of environmental filtering. This phenomenon reflects the rate at which species are exchanged along designated ecological gradients. Furthermore, nestedness is characterized by a specific pattern of species composition, whereby a less diverse site is a strict subset of a more diverse site. The calculations for these metrics will utilize the formulas established by Podani.

In [43]:
from itertools import combinations

def unique_combinations(lst):
    # Validate input
    if not isinstance(lst, list):
        raise TypeError("Input must be a list.")
    if len(lst) < 2:
        return []  # No pairs possible

    # Generate all unique 2-element combinations
    return list(combinations(lst, 2))

# Example usage
data = list(df_sp['site'].unique())
pairs = unique_combinations(data)

dict_PODANI = {}

source = gdf_sp.set_index('site')
for pair in pairs:

    site_1 = pair[0]
    site_2 = pair[1]
    index = f"{site_1}-{site_2}"
    
    a = source.loc[site_1,'species']
    b = source.loc[site_2,'species']
    
    alfa_a = len(a)
    alfa_b = len(b)
    
    overlap = sorted([item for item in a if item in b])
    unique_a_list = sorted(list(set(a) - set(b)))
    unique_b_list = sorted(list(set(b) - set(a)))
    
    unique_a = alfa_a - len(overlap)
    unique_b = alfa_b - len(overlap)
    
    gamma = unique_a + unique_b + len(overlap)
    
    Similarity = round(len(overlap) / gamma,3)
    Replacement = round((min([unique_a,unique_b]) * 2) / gamma,3)
    Nestedeness = round((len(overlap) + abs(unique_a - unique_b)) / gamma,2)


    dict_PODANI[index] = {'Similarity':Similarity,'Replacement':Replacement,'Nestedeness':Nestedeness}


df_PODANI = pd.DataFrame(data=dict_PODANI).T
df_PODANI.index.name = 'Sites comparison'

df_PODANI
Out[43]:
Similarity Replacement Nestedeness
Sites comparison
1-2 0.125 0.500 0.50
1-3 0.286 0.286 0.71
1-4 0.300 0.000 1.00
1-5 0.154 0.154 0.85
1-6 0.500 0.500 0.50
... ... ... ...
99-101 0.133 0.667 0.33
99-102 0.231 0.615 0.38
100-101 0.333 0.333 0.67
100-102 0.250 0.500 0.50
101-102 0.462 0.462 0.54

5151 rows × 3 columns

GIS helps us evaluate distances and the structure of landscapes in the data. The first analytical step was to calculate the distance matrix among the observed points, using QGIS. Regarding landscape structure, I utilized resources from the open-source community. I employed the European Space Agency (ESA) WorldCover dataset, which offers a global land cover map for 2021 at a 10-meter resolution, based on data from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 satellites. I clipped relevant areas by calculating a buffer of 500 meters around each point and exported the raster image using Google Earth Engine; however, I restricted the output dimensions to 50 meters due to storage limitations.

In [44]:
pd_matrix_distance = gpd.read_file(r"C:/Users/Luigi/OneDrive/Documenti/Elskenecologie/Carabidae/distance_matrix_2023_new.geojson")
pd_matrix_distance['Sites comparison'] = pd_matrix_distance.apply(lambda x: f"{x['InputID']}-{x['TargetID']}",axis=1)
pd_matrix_distance.set_index('Sites comparison',inplace=True)
pd_matrix_distance.drop('geometry',axis=1,inplace=True)
In [45]:
gdf_landuse = gpd.read_file(r"C:/Users/Luigi/OneDrive/Documenti/Elskenecologie/Carabidae/carabidae_hist_2023_3.geojson").drop('geometry',axis=1)
columns = ['site',"Tree cover","Grassland","Cropland","Built-up","Bare/sparse vegetation","Permanent water bodies","Herbaceous wetland"] 
gdf_landuse.columns = columns

df = gdf_landuse

df.iloc[:, 1:] = df.iloc[:, 1:].apply(lambda x: x.div(x.sum()).mul(100), axis=1).round(1)
df['site'] = df['site'].astype(str)

df.set_index('site',inplace=True)

dict_NDVI = {}

for type in ["Tree cover","Grassland","Cropland","Built-up","Bare/sparse vegetation","Permanent water bodies","Herbaceous wetland"]: 
    temp_list = []
    for index_couple in df_PODANI.index:
        a = index_couple.split('-')[0]
        b = index_couple.split('-')[1]
        temp_list.append(round(abs(df.loc[a,f'{type}'] - df.loc[b,f'{type}']),2))
    
    dict_NDVI[f'{type}'] = temp_list

df_PODANI = df_PODANI.join(pd_matrix_distance)[['Similarity', 'Replacement','Nestedeness','Distance',]]
df_PODANI = pd.concat([df_PODANI,pd.DataFrame(data=dict_NDVI,index=df_PODANI.index)],axis=1)

df_PODANI

In [66]:
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_california_housing
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score, root_mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error

# Step 1: Load dataset
data = fetch_california_housing()
X = df_PODANI[[ 'Distance',
       'Tree cover', 'Grassland', 'Cropland', 'Built-up',
       'Bare/sparse vegetation', 'Permanent water bodies',
       'Herbaceous wetland']].to_numpy()
y = df_PODANI['Similarity'].to_numpy()

# Step 2: Train-test split
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3)

# Step 3: Scale features
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

# # Step 3: Scale features
# scaler = StandardScaler()
# X_train_scaled = X_train
# X_test_scaled = X_test

dict_r2 = {}
for depth in list(range(1,20)):
    
    # Step 4: Train Random Forest model
    model = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=10, max_depth=depth)
    model.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
    predictions = model.predict(X_test_scaled)
    r2 = round(r2_score(y_test, predictions),5)
    dict_r2[depth] = r2


df_depth = pd.DataFrame(data=dict_r2.items(),columns=['Depth','R2']).set_index('Depth')

df_depth['R2'].max()

max_depth = df_depth[df_depth['R2'] == df_depth['R2'].max()].index[0]

# Sample data
data = df_depth.reset_index()


# Column to highlight
highlight_col = int(max_depth)

# Create bar chart with conditional color
chart = alt.Chart(data).mark_bar().encode(
    x=alt.X('Depth:N', title=None),
    y=alt.Y('R2:Q', title=None),
    color=alt.condition(
        alt.datum.Depth == highlight_col,  
        alt.value('red'),                  
        alt.value('lightgrey')                 
    )
).properties(
    title=f"Best depth: {highlight_col} (R2 {round(df_depth['R2'].max(),3)})",
    width=400,
    height=300
)

chart

feature_names = [ 'Distance',
       'Tree cover', 'Grassland', 'Cropland', 'Built-up',
       'Bare/sparse vegetation', 'Permanent water bodies',
       'Herbaceous wetland']

model = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=10, max_depth=highlight_col)
model.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

importances = model.feature_importances_
feature_imp_df = pd.DataFrame({'Feature': feature_names, 'Gini Importance': importances}).sort_values(
    'Gini Importance', ascending=False)

# Sample data
data = feature_imp_df


# # Column to highlight
# highlight_col = int(max_depth)

# Create bar chart with conditional color
chart = alt.Chart(data).mark_bar().encode(
    x=alt.X('Feature:N', title=None),
    y=alt.Y('Gini Importance:Q', title=None),
    color=alt.condition(
        alt.datum.Depth == highlight_col,  
        alt.value('red'),                  
        alt.value('lightgrey')                 
    )
).properties(
    title=f"Best depth: {highlight_col} (R2 {round(df_depth['R2'].max(),3)})",
    width=400,
    height=300
)

chart
